hero.title: Mastery Guide: Historical Underpinnings & Fundamental Rights
hero.subtitle: Comprehensive coverage of British-era Constitutional Acts (1773-1947) and Part III (Articles 12-35) of the Indian Constitution.
hero.whyItMatters: The British-era Acts form the structural foundation of our Constitution (especially the 1935 Act), while Fundamental Rights (Part III) are the most frequently tested articles in SSC CGL. Mastering both guarantees securing critical marks in Polity.

quickFacts.fact: First Step: The Regulating Act of 1773 was the first step taken by the British Parliament to control the East India Company.
quickFacts.heading: 1773 Act

quickFacts.fact: First Supreme Court: Established at Calcutta in 1774 under the Regulating Act of 1773, with Sir Elijah Impey as Chief Justice.
quickFacts.heading: Supreme Court

quickFacts.fact: Crown Rule: The Government of India Act 1858 transferred power directly to the British Crown, ending Company rule.
quickFacts.heading: Crown Rule

quickFacts.fact: Communal Electorate: Introduced by the Indian Councils Act 1909 (Minto-Morley Reforms) for Muslims.
quickFacts.heading: Communal Electorate

quickFacts.fact: Blueprint: The Government of India Act 1935 provided the primary structural blueprint for the Indian Constitution.
quickFacts.heading: 1935 Act

quickFacts.fact: Magna Carta: Part III of the Constitution (Fundamental Rights) is rightly described as the 'Magna Carta of India'.
quickFacts.heading: Magna Carta

quickFacts.fact: Citizens Only: Articles 15, 16, 19, 29, and 30 are available EXCLUSIVELY to Indian citizens.
quickFacts.heading: Citizens Only

quickFacts.fact: Un-suspendable: Articles 20 and 21 cannot be suspended even during a National Emergency (Article 352).
quickFacts.heading: Un-suspendable

quickFacts.fact: Property Right: The Right to Property (Article 31) was deleted from Fundamental Rights by the 44th Amendment Act (1978).
quickFacts.heading: Property Right

quickFacts.fact: Judicial Review: Article 13 provides the basis for Judicial Review, making laws inconsistent with FRs void.
quickFacts.heading: Judicial Review

quickFacts.fact: Heart & Soul: Article 32 is called the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar.
quickFacts.heading: Heart & Soul

quickFacts.fact: RTE: Article 21A (Right to Education) was added by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act in 2002.
quickFacts.heading: Right to Education

notes.heading: 1. The Company Rule (1773–1858)
notes.explanation: This period marks the gradual centralization of power in India by the British Parliament over the East India Company. The Regulating Act (1773) designated the Governor of Bengal as the 'Governor-General of Bengal' (Warren Hastings). Pitt's India Act (1784) created a system of double government (Board of Control for political affairs, Court of Directors for commercial affairs). The Charter Act of 1833 was the final step towards centralization, making the GG of Bengal the 'Governor-General of India' (William Bentinck). The Charter Act of 1853 separated the legislative and executive functions of the GG's council.
keyTakeaways: 1773 Act: Established the Supreme Court at Calcutta (1774).
1813 Act: Ended the Company's trade monopoly in India (except for tea and trade with China).
1833 Act: Ended all commercial activities of the EIC; introduced an open competition system for civil servants (only on paper).
1853 Act: Successfully introduced the open competition system (Macaulay Committee, 1854).
importantExamPoints: Charter Act 1833 deprived the Governors of Bombay and Madras of their legislative powers.
Pitt's India Act 1784 distinguished between the commercial and political functions of the Company.
commonStudentMistakes: Confusing 1813 and 1833: 1813 ended monopoly *except* tea/China. 1833 ended it *completely*.
Confusing Supreme Courts: The SC established in 1774 (Regulating Act) is different from the Federal Court established in 1937 (GoI Act 1935).

notes.heading: 2. The Crown Rule (1858–1947)
notes.explanation: Post the 1857 Revolt, the GoI Act 1858 transferred powers to the British Crown. The designation of GG of India was changed to 'Viceroy of India' (Lord Canning). A new office, 'Secretary of State for India', was created. The Indian Councils Act 1909 (Minto-Morley Reforms) introduced a system of communal representation. The GoI Act 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms) introduced 'Dyarchy' (dual rule) at the provinces and bicameralism at the center. The GoI Act 1935 introduced 'Provincial Autonomy', abolished dyarchy in provinces, but introduced it at the center.
keyTakeaways: 1858 Act: Known as the Act for the Good Government of India.
1909 Act: Lord Minto became known as the Father of Communal Electorate.
1919 Act: Separated central and provincial subjects; introduced direct elections.
1935 Act: Established a Federal Court, RBI, and divided powers into Federal, Provincial, and Concurrent lists.
importantExamPoints: GoI Act 1935 is the most significant source of the Indian Constitution (administrative details, federal scheme, judiciary).
The Indian Independence Act 1947 declared India an independent and sovereign state.
commonStudentMistakes: Mixing Minto-Morley (1909) and Montagu-Chelmsford (1919): 1909 = Communal Electorate; 1919 = Dyarchy in Provinces.
Misunderstanding Dyarchy: Dyarchy in 1919 meant dividing provincial subjects into 'Transferred' and 'Reserved'. In 1935, this was moved to the Center.

notes.heading: 3. Introduction to Fundamental Rights (Articles 12-13)
notes.explanation: Enshrined in Part III (Articles 12 to 35), Fundamental Rights are borrowed from the US Constitution's Bill of Rights. They are justiciable, meaning they can be enforced in courts. However, they are not absolute and are subject to 'reasonable restrictions'. Article 12 defines 'The State', which includes the GoI, Parliament, State Governments, and all local/other authorities. Article 13 declares that laws inconsistent with or in derogation of Fundamental Rights shall be void, establishing the doctrine of Judicial Review.
keyTakeaways: Article 12: Broad definition of 'State' against which FRs can be enforced.
Article 13: Gives teeth to the FRs via Judicial Review (empowers SC and HCs).
FRs strike a balance between individual liberty and social control.
importantExamPoints: Fundamental Rights guarantee political democracy, whereas DPSP guarantees social and economic democracy.
Amendments to the Constitution can be challenged under Article 13 if they violate the 'Basic Structure'.
commonStudentMistakes: Thinking FRs are absolute: They are not. The state can impose reasonable restrictions (e.g., for security, public order).
Assuming Article 12 is a right: Article 12 just defines what constitutes the 'State'.

notes.heading: 4. Right to Equality (Art 14-18) & Right to Freedom (Art 19-22)
notes.explanation: Right to Equality ensures equal treatment: Art 14 (Equality before law), Art 15 (No discrimination on religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth), Art 16 (Equality in public employment), Art 17 (Abolition of untouchability), Art 18 (Abolition of titles). Right to Freedom guarantees civil liberties: Art 19 guarantees 6 freedoms (speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, profession). Art 20 protects in respect of conviction for offenses (no ex-post-facto law, no double jeopardy). Art 21 guarantees life and personal liberty. Art 22 protects against arrest and detention.
keyTakeaways: Art 17 (Untouchability) and Art 24 (Child labor) are absolute rights.
Art 21A: Right to free and compulsory education for ages 6-14 (86th Amendment, 2002).
Art 19 freedoms can be suspended during National Emergency (war/external aggression) under Art 358.
importantExamPoints: Article 20 and 21 CANNOT be suspended during ANY emergency (44th Amendment Act, 1978).
Titles like Bharat Ratna or Padma Vibhushan are not considered 'titles' under Article 18.
commonStudentMistakes: Forgetting the 5 grounds of Art 15: Religion, Race, Caste, Sex, Place of Birth (RRCS-P). Residence is NOT a ground under Art 15, but it is under Art 16.
Confusing Preventive vs Punitive detention in Article 22.

notes.heading: 5. Rights Against Exploitation, Religion, Culture & Remedies (Art 23-35)
notes.explanation: Right against Exploitation: Art 23 (Prohibits human trafficking and forced labor/begar) and Art 24 (Prohibits child labor below 14 years). Right to Religion (Art 25-28): Freedom of conscience, managing religious affairs, and freedom from religious taxes/instruction. Cultural and Educational Rights (Art 29-30): Protection of minority interests and their right to establish educational institutions. Right to Constitutional Remedies (Art 32): Empowers the Supreme Court to issue 5 types of Writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo-Warranto) for the enforcement of FRs.
keyTakeaways: Art 29 & 30 are exclusively for religious and linguistic minorities.
Article 33 empowers Parliament to restrict FRs of armed forces, police, etc., to ensure discipline.
Article 34 restricts FRs while martial law is in force in any area.
importantExamPoints: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called Article 32 the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution.
Habeas Corpus ('to have the body') can be issued against both public authorities and private individuals.
commonStudentMistakes: Thinking minorities are defined in the Constitution: The Constitution mentions religious and linguistic minorities but DOES NOT define the term 'minority'.
Confusing Prohibition and Certiorari: Prohibition is issued BEFORE an order is passed (preventive); Certiorari is issued AFTER to quash an order (curative).

timeline.title: Regulating Act
timeline.shortDescription: First intervention of British Parliament; created GG of Bengal.
timeline.importance: Foundation of central administration in India.

timeline.title: Charter Act
timeline.shortDescription: GG of Bengal became GG of India; centralized administration.
timeline.importance: Complete end of EIC's commercial monopoly.

timeline.title: Government of India Act
timeline.shortDescription: Transferred power from EIC to British Crown; created Viceroy.
timeline.importance: Began the era of direct British Crown Rule.

timeline.title: Government of India Act (Mont-Ford)
timeline.shortDescription: Introduced Dyarchy in provinces and Bicameralism at center.
timeline.importance: First introduction of direct elections in India.

timeline.title: Government of India Act
timeline.importance: Major structural blueprint for the 1950 Indian Constitution.

timeline.title: Constitution Enforced
timeline.shortDescription: Fundamental Rights (Part III) come into force.
timeline.importance: India becomes a Republic with guaranteed civil liberties.

timeline.title: 44th Amendment Act
timeline.shortDescription: Deleted Right to Property from Fundamental Rights.
timeline.importance: Property right downgraded to a mere legal right (Article 300A).

personalities.name: Warren Hastings
personalities.majorContributions: Appointed under the Regulating Act 1773. Laid the foundation of civil administration in India.

personalities.name: Lord William Bentinck
personalities.majorContributions: Appointed under the Charter Act 1833. Known for abolishing Sati and introducing English education.

personalities.name: Lord Canning
personalities.majorContributions: GG during the 1857 revolt, became the first Viceroy under the GoI Act 1858.

personalities.name: Lord Minto
personalities.majorContributions: Known as the 'Father of Communal Electorate' for the 1909 Reforms.

terms.definition: Derived from Greek 'di-arche' meaning double rule. Introduced in provinces in 1919, dividing executive subjects into 'Transferred' (popular ministers) and 'Reserved' (Governor).
A system where seats are reserved for a specific religious community, and only members of that community can vote for their candidates (Introduced in 1909).
Capable of being enforced by a court of law. Fundamental Rights are justiciable; if violated, citizens can move the courts directly.
A writ meaning 'We Command'. Issued by a court to a public official asking them to perform their official duties that they have failed or refused to perform.
A legal principle (Article 20(2)) stating no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offense more than once.
A law that imposes penalties for an act that was not illegal when it was committed. Article 20(1) prohibits retrospective criminal laws.

tables.title: Evolution of Top Executive Posts in British India
Classification of Fundamental Rights (Articles 14-32)
rows: Civil liberties, protection in conviction, life/liberty.
Banning human trafficking, begar, child labor.
Enforcement of rights via Supreme Court Writs.

tables.title: Writs Issued under Article 32 / 226
rows: Direct public official to perform duty (Public only).
Prevent lower court from exceeding jurisdiction (Preventive).
Quash the order of a lower court/tribunal (Curative).
Check illegal usurpation of a public office by a person.

memoryTricks.mnemonic: E F E R C E
memoryTricks.explanation: E = Equality (14-18), F = Freedom (19-22), E = Exploitation (23-24), R = Religion (25-28), C = Cultural/Edu (29-30), E = Enforcement/Remedies (32).
Religion, Race, Caste, Sex, Place of birth. (Note: 'Residence' is NOT included in Art 15, but is in Art 16 for employment).

memoryTricks.mnemonic: S A A M R P
memoryTricks.explanation: Speech, Assembly, Association, Movement, Residence, Profession.
Minto-Morley was 1909 (Shorter name, earlier year). Montagu-Chelmsford was 1919 (Longer name, later year).

examTraps.correction: Remember: 1909 = Communal Electorate (Muslims). 1919 = Dyarchy in Provinces & Bicameralism at Center.

examTraps.trap: Assuming Dyarchy meant the same thing at the same level.
examTraps.correction: In 1919, Dyarchy was introduced in the PROVINCES. In 1935, Provincial Dyarchy was ABOLISHED, and Dyarchy was introduced at the CENTER.

examTraps.trap: Thinking Article 19 is suspended during any emergency.
examTraps.correction: Article 19 can ONLY be suspended when National Emergency is declared on grounds of 'War or External Aggression'. It CANNOT be suspended on grounds of 'Armed Rebellion' (internal emergency).

examTraps.trap: Assuming all FRs apply only to citizens.
examTraps.correction: Articles 15, 16, 19, 29, 30 are strictly for Indian Citizens. Art 14 (Equality), Art 21 (Life), Art 21A (Education) apply to BOTH citizens and foreigners.

examTraps.trap: Confusing the writs of Mandamus and Prohibition.
examTraps.correction: Mandamus tells a public official to 'DO' something (act). Prohibition tells a lower court 'NOT TO DO' something (stop acting).

quiz.q: Which of the following Acts established the Supreme Court at Calcutta?
quiz.explanation: The Regulating Act of 1773 provided for the establishment of a Supreme Court at Calcutta (1774) comprising one chief justice and three other judges.

quiz.q: Who was designated as the first Governor-General of India?
quiz.explanation: The Charter Act of 1833 made the Governor-General of Bengal the 'Governor-General of India'. Lord William Bentinck was the first to hold this title.

quiz.q: The system of 'Communal Electorate' in India was first introduced by which Act?
quiz.explanation: The Indian Councils Act of 1909 (Minto-Morley Reforms) introduced a system of communal representation for Muslims.

quiz.q: Which Act abolished 'Dyarchy' in the provinces and introduced 'Provincial Autonomy'?
quiz.explanation: The GoI Act 1935 abolished the dyarchy introduced in 1919 in the provinces and replaced it with 'provincial autonomy'.

quiz.q: The Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution are inspired by the Constitution of which country?
quiz.explanation: Part III of the Constitution (Fundamental Rights) draws its inspiration from the Bill of Rights of the USA.

quiz.q: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available ONLY to citizens of India and NOT to foreigners?
quiz.explanation: Articles 15, 16, 19, 29, and 30 are available exclusively to Indian citizens. Articles 14, 21, and 25 are available to both.

quiz.q: Article 17 of the Indian Constitution deals with the abolition of:
quiz.explanation: Article 17 abolishes 'untouchability' and forbids its practice in any form. It is an absolute right.

quiz.q: By which Constitutional Amendment was the Right to Property deleted from the list of Fundamental Rights?
quiz.explanation: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) deleted Article 31 and made the Right to Property a legal right under Article 300A in Part XII.

quiz.q: Which Article of the Constitution guarantees the right to free and compulsory education for children aged 6 to 14?
quiz.explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Amendment Act in 2002, makes elementary education a Fundamental Right.

quiz.q: Which two Articles CANNOT be suspended even during a National Emergency?
quiz.explanation: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) mandated that the President cannot suspend the right to move the Court for the enforcement of fundamental rights guaranteed by Articles 20 and 21.

quiz.q: Which writ translates to 'We Command'?
quiz.explanation: Mandamus literally means 'we command'. It is a command issued by the court to a public official asking him to perform his official duties.

quiz.q: The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919) are famous for introducing which of the following features?
quiz.explanation: The GoI Act 1919 (Mont-Ford Reforms) introduced a dual scheme of governance in the provinces known as 'dyarchy' (divided into transferred and reserved subjects).

quiz.q: Which Act created the office of the 'Secretary of State for India'?
quiz.explanation: The GoI Act 1858 abolished the Board of Control and Court of Directors and created a new office, Secretary of State for India, vested with complete authority.

quiz.q: Article 24 of the Indian Constitution strictly prohibits:
options: Employment of children below 14 years in factories
quiz.explanation: Article 24 prohibits the employment of children below the age of 14 years in any factory, mine, or other hazardous activities.

quiz.q: What does the Latin writ term 'Quo-Warranto' mean?
quiz.explanation: Quo-Warranto is issued by the court to inquire into the legality of a claim of a person to a public office, preventing illegal usurpation.

quiz.q: Which of the following rights was considered by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar as the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution?
options: Right to Life and Personal Liberty
quiz.explanation: Dr. Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the heart and soul because it makes all other fundamental rights enforceable.

quiz.q: The doctrine of 'Judicial Review' in India is explicitly based on which Article of the Constitution?
quiz.explanation: Article 13 declares that all laws that are inconsistent with or in derogation of any of the fundamental rights shall be void, which is the basis of Judicial Review.

quiz.q: Which Act separated, for the first time, the legislative and executive functions of the Governor-General’s council?
quiz.explanation: The Charter Act of 1853 established a separate Governor-General’s legislative council which came to be known as the Indian (Central) Legislative Council.

quiz.q: Protection against 'Double Jeopardy' is guaranteed under which Article?
quiz.explanation: Article 20(2) states that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offense more than once (double jeopardy).

quiz.q: The establishment of a Federal Court in India was recommended by:
quiz.explanation: The GoI Act 1935 provided for the establishment of a Federal Court, which was set up in 1937.

flashcards.front: Which Act made the Governor of Bengal the Governor-General of Bengal?
Who was the first Governor-General of India?
Which Act ended the Company's rule and transferred power to the Crown?
Which Act is known as the Minto-Morley Reforms?
What major feature was introduced by the Minto-Morley Reforms?
Which Act introduced 'Dyarchy' in the provinces?
Which Act provided the major structural blueprint for the Indian Constitution?
Fundamental Rights (Part III) are inspired by the constitution of which country?
Which Article provides the definition of 'State'?
Which Article is the basis for Judicial Review?
What does Article 14 guarantee?
flashcards.back: Equality before the law and equal protection of laws.

flashcards.front: Which Articles are exclusively available to Indian citizens?
Which Article abolishes Untouchability?
How many freedoms are guaranteed under Article 19?
flashcards.back: 6 Freedoms (Speech, Assembly, Association, Movement, Residence, Profession).

flashcards.front: Which two Articles cannot be suspended during an Emergency?
Which Article prohibits human trafficking and forced labor (begar)?
Which Article prohibits child labor below 14 years?
Which Article guarantees the right of minorities to establish educational institutions?
Which Article is known as the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution?
What does the writ of Habeas Corpus mean?
flashcards.back: 'To have the body of' (protection against illegal detention).

revisionSheet.title: Historical Underpinnings & Fundamental Rights Revision
points: 1773 Regulating Act: 1st step by British, SC at Calcutta.
1833 Charter: William Bentinck (1st GG of India).
1858 GoI Act: Company rule ends, Crown takes over, Canning (1st Viceroy).
1935 Act: Blueprint of Constitution, Provincial Autonomy.
Part III: Articles 12-35 (Fundamental Rights). Borrowed from USA.
Art 12: Definition of State. Art 13: Judicial Review.
Religion (25-28): Freedom of conscience and practice.
Remedies (32): Writs (SC). Heart and Soul of Constitution.
Writs: Habeas Corpus (Body), Mandamus (Command), Prohibition (Forbid), Certiorari (Certify), Quo-Warranto (Authority).
Emergency Exceptions: Arts 20 & 21 never suspend. Art 19 suspends only on War/External Aggression.
Property Right: Deleted from FRs by 44th Amendment (1978); now legal right (300A).
